title: My Explanation and the Questions I Raise!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The printed source itself contains a short mojibake-like character sequence immediately after an English Cabinet-note quotation. Because the intended characters are not visually recoverable, that source artifact is preserved in the Tamil reading copy and no Tamil text is invented for it. The canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: They say anger is the enemy of reason. Those holding offices such as Chief Minister should especially avoid anger. I have raised many issues concerning the Tamil Nadu government and expressed my views. But I made no accusation whatsoever against the state government over the “Model Schools.”

Neither in my Question and Answer column on 31 October 2013 nor in my Udanpirappu letter of 5 November did I say anything blaming the AIADMK government in Tamil Nadu while discussing the Union government's proposed Model Schools. What I wrote on 31 October was this:

> **Question:** There are reports that, under the Union government's Rashtriya Adarsh Vidyalaya scheme, the Union government and private parties are joining to establish Model Schools, commercialising school education in Tamil Nadu. > > **Kalaignar:** This is another attempt by the Union government to interfere in the authority of the state. Without any partnership with private parties, the Tamil Nadu government itself has established Model Schools in educationally backward districts. Yet the Union government has now invited private parties to apply to establish Model Schools in areas that are not educationally backward and appears ready to decide upon those applications. > > The state government has been completely bypassed. Allowing the Union government directly to permit privatisation in education under the name of PPP, without the state government's approval or consultation, would set a bad precedent. State governments, it seems, are merely to arrange land for private parties to run the schools. The state government must not permit this intrusion by the Union government into its authority. The Tamil Nadu government must immediately register its opposition.
Did I write anything there accusing the state government? I wrote only that the Union government's intrusion into state authority must not be permitted and that the state government should oppose it.

I waited a few days to see whether the state would object. In my Udanpirappu letter of 5 November, I noted that after I expressed my view, leaders of other parties in Tamil Nadu had also said land should not be provided for the Union government's Model Schools. In particular, PMK founder Dr Ramadoss, Dravidar Kazhagam president Tamilar Thalaivar K. Veeramani, Tamil Nadu Elementary School Teachers' Federation General Secretary K. Meenakshisundaram, and State Platform for Common School System General Secretary P. B. Gajendrababu had issued detailed statements. But the Tamil Nadu School Education Department had not opened its mouth. I merely asked whether the state government would at least now come forward to express its opposition.

The Chief Minister replied on 10 November to what I had written on 31 October and 5 November. Was it truly a reply? Under the name of a reply, she freely and shamelessly used the uncultured and indecent language characteristic of her.
Her statement said:

> While in office, Karunanidhi not only kept his mouth closed and remained silent about the Union government's Rashtriya Adarsh Vidyalaya scheme for selfish reasons, but supported and implemented it in Tamil Nadu. For him now to issue an elaborate statement under the heading, “Will the state government come forward to oppose the Union government?”, is the height of fraudulence.

The Model Schools approved under the DMK government are being implemented in Tamil Nadu through Rashtriya Madhyamik Shiksha Abhiyan, the programme for universal secondary education. The scheme the Union government now proposes is Rashtriya Adarsh Vidyalaya. The project begun in Tamil Nadu under the DMK government gave no role to private parties. It is only the present Union-government scheme that opens the door to them. I was pointing to the privatisation of school education. Dr Ramadoss made the same point.

In yesterday's statement, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa acknowledged that the Union government wrote to the Tamil Nadu government on 19 November 2008 about such a scheme, and that Karunanidhi, then Chief Minister, wrote by hand on the file, “May be discussed in the Cabinet.” She further said the Cabinet decided, “After detailed discussion, the Cabinet has approved the School Education Department's proposal to establish twenty Model Schools in educationally backward blocks of Tamil Nadu with Union-government assistance at an estimated cost of Rs 75.40 crore.”

Jayalalithaa's own words establish that the Union government did not decide to open those Model Schools without consulting the state government, and that the DMK government did not approve their establishment through private participation.

What I said now was only that permitting the Union government directly to privatise education under the name PPP, without the approval or consultation of the state government, would set a bad precedent. I made no accusation against the Tamil Nadu government. I asked it to express its opposition to the Union government.

Jayalalithaa admits that the matter was discussed by the Cabinet under the DMK government. But in her statement she cited only the second paragraph of that Cabinet note. This is what one calls grazing only at the tips of the grass! Should she not have read what the other paragraphs said?
The first paragraph of the Cabinet note states:

> The Government of India have launched a Scheme to open 6000 Model Schools in Educationally Backward Blocks in all the States during the 11th Five Year Plan. These Schools will be established with stipulations on pupil-teacher ratio, Information and Communication Technology (ICT) usage, holistic educational environment, appropriate curriculum with infrastructure facilities of a standard as in Kendriya Vidyalaya. The salient features of scheme are as follows: > > Locations: 2500 Model Schools will be set up in Educationally Backward Blocks. > > Land: Land for these schools will be identified and provided by the State Governments.

Nowhere does this say anything about private intrusion. There was therefore no question of the DMK government accepting such intrusion. At that Cabinet meeting, the DMK government approved the establishment by the Tamil Nadu government itself of twenty Model Schools with Union assistance. Even then, the state government did not surrender complete authority to the Union government.

Paragraph 3 of the same Cabinet note records that in January 2009 a proposal was sent to the Union government to open twenty Model Schools in educationally backward blocks of Tamil Nadu. The total estimate was Rs 75.40 crore, comprising the Union government's 75 per cent share of Rs 65.65 crore and the state government's 25 per cent share of Rs 18.85 crore. It thus made clear that not even one per cent was allotted to private parties.
The same Cabinet note also said:
> The Planning Commission was informed that the Model Schools to be opened in Tamil Nadu will follow State Syllabus and Curriculum and will be affiliated to the State Board of Examinations.

In other words, it clearly stated that the Model Schools opened in Tamil Nadu would follow the state syllabus. Does it say anywhere that private participation or a Union-government curriculum would be introduced? Should Chief Minister Jayalalithaa not have read all this fully before setting out to abuse me? *Janasakthi*, which published Jayalalithaa's statement in banner headlines on its front page, should uphold journalistic ethics by publishing this explanation of mine too. Will it come forward to do so?

Jayalalithaa's statement next says: “It is noteworthy that only English-medium education will be provided in these schools. Karunanidhi, who now speaks until his mouth tears about English-medium education, must explain why he approved this scheme then.” Even the Amma who gave birth to me would not have called me “Karunanidhi, Karunanidhi” so many times with such a full mouth!

Let me again point Jayalalithaa to the very Cabinet note. Should a person holding the office of Chief Minister not exercise care when issuing a statement in her own name? Had she calmly read even the note she cited, without anger, she would not have abused me to this extent.
The Cabinet note clearly states:

> Medium of Instructions: The medium of instructions will be decided by the State Government. Classes: The schools will have classes from IX to XII if the medium of instruction is the regional language, or VI to XII, if the medium of instruction is English.

Jayalalithaa, who studied in an English-medium school, must explain whether “regional language” means English. Nowhere does the Cabinet note say that only English-medium education would be provided. We can therefore see that the Chief Minister's claim that those Model Schools would teach only in English is contrary to fact.

What I have set out shows that the DMK government surrendered none of the state's authority. Even now, I merely pointed out that the Union government must not be permitted to seize the state's powers. For that, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has leapt upon me and clawed away in this fashion.

Jayalalithaa further said in her statement: “Karunanidhi, who acted against state autonomy while ruling the state, now speaking about state autonomy is like Satan quoting scripture.” My speaking about state autonomy is apparently Satan quoting scripture. Who says this? What kind of...? The dignity I learned from Anna restrains my pen!

It was I who, at the Tiruchirappalli conference in 1970, raised “Autonomy in the State; Federalism at the Centre” as one of our five great slogans. It was I who conducted a conference as early as 1972 to insist that the Union government must not seize the powers of the states. Under the DMK government, I was the first in India to establish an expert committee headed by Justice Rajamannar to study state autonomy and submit a report. My beloved Kanmani Murasoli Maran wrote a book titled *State Autonomy* at that very time.

After the Union government's Model School announcement, statements were issued not only by me but also by PMK founder Dr Ramadoss, Tamilar Thalaivar K. Veeramani and many educationists. Dr Ramadoss said with good intentions:

> When the Union government previously announced such Model Schools in educationally backward unions, the Tamil Nadu government itself established and has been running them without permitting private parties. In the same way, the Tamil Nadu government itself should establish and operate the 356 Model Schools now announced.
Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has accused him too.
Jayalalithaa further says:

> In educationally backward unions, the Union government's scheme was that Model Schools should be established with contributions from the Union and state governments. Accordingly, the Tamil Nadu government itself established them. But the present Union-government scheme is that Model Schools in unions or areas that are not educationally backward should be established under the public-private model.
Her own words show that “public-private model” did not appear in the earlier scheme implemented under the DMK government, while it does appear in the current scheme.

Jayalalithaa writes, “Karunanidhi seems to have forgotten that through my statement I had already pointed out that the Union government's action is against state autonomy.” Can she say when, and on what date after the Union government's Model School announcement, she issued a statement calling the action contrary to state autonomy? Had she issued such a statement, there would have been no need for me to demand that the Tamil Nadu government oppose the Union government's decision.

Jayalalithaa also says it appears that not one of the forty-one Model Schools initially established in India under the Union government's public-private scheme is in Tamil Nadu, and that no one from Tamil Nadu participated. It would have been enough for Chief Minister Jayalalithaa or the minister concerned to state this single fact immediately after our statements. We would have concluded that Tamil Nadu faced no harm.

Instead, the Chief Minister unnecessarily prepares for a pointless quarrel and a vulgar song-and-dance. When I issue a statement on behalf of the AIADMK government too, out of concern that Tamil Nadu should not be harmed, Jayalalithaa replies that Karunanidhi's statement fits the proverb, “The frog perishes by its own mouth.” Is this politics?

When the DMK was in opposition, Perarignar Anna told the Congress Chief Minister in the Assembly, while seeking schemes from the Union government for Tamil Nadu: “Honourable Chief Minister, do not think I am criticising you. Point to me before the Union government and obtain what Tamil Nadu needs. I will only be happy.” Today's Chief Minister may not know that Anna spoke in this way then.
The Chief Minister has issued such a long statement in response to one I made to help the Tamil Nadu government. But I have already put many questions to her. When will she answer them?
I asked when the stalled Sethusamudram project would see deliverance. What is her answer?

The Union minister and the National Highways Authority have filled page after page describing the Tamil Nadu government's delays over the Maduravoyal–Port elevated expressway and many other road projects in the state. What is her answer to them?

A Union minister accused the Tamil Nadu government of failing properly to implement the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme and of allowing many irregularities within it. What is her answer?

I pointed out that an increase in the Family Security Fund paid to the heirs of deceased pensioners had already been announced in the Assembly and ordered by the government the previous year, but that the Chief Minister presented it as a new announcement. I asked her to explain that confusion. Where is the answer?

I asked for answers to the many questions surrounding the arrest of alleged extremists such as Police Fakruddin and the promotions and cash rewards announced by the Chief Minister for police personnel. Where are the answers?
How many days have passed since I asked for an explanation of the rising prices of sand, cement and other construction materials? Where is the answer?
Where is the explanation for sand theft, mineral theft and beach-mineral theft?

*The Hindu Tamil* reported one day that video evidence existed concerning a Housing Board official in Erode in connection with Housing Board land dealings. He died by suicide the next day. What became of that case?

The Chief Minister herself said the Union government's Food Security Act would harm Tamil Nadu and cause a loss of several hundred crore rupees. Many days have passed since a Union minister replied that the Act would instead save Tamil Nadu Rs 600 crore. Where is her response?
Farmers are demanding higher procurement prices for paddy and sugarcane. What is her answer?
The problems of farmers, fishers, weavers and every section of society grow by the day. Where are the answers?
The courts have levelled accusation after accusation against the Teachers Recruitment Board, matters shameful even to hear about. Where is the answer?
The government announced a pay rise for public employees, yet many complained that their pay had fallen below what they already received. Where is the explanation?
I asked about this government's full-page advertisements, how much was allotted for them this year and how much was actually spent. Why has she not answered?
Many days have passed since the satellite-town announcement. I have long asked, “Where is that town?” What is the answer?
The twelfth wage settlement for Transport Corporation employees should have been concluded in August. Many months have passed. What is the answer?
What became of the “Your Own Home” scheme announced for police personnel? How many have received homes so far? Where is the explanation?
Should she not have answered the report headlined “A Pot of Water Costs Five Rupees in Chennai”?
The dismissed People's Welfare Workers continue their protests. Why this vindictive action against them?
Day after day, reports pile up of murder, robbery, highway robbery and chain-snatching. What is her answer to all this?
Reports keep coming of corruption everywhere, corruption in everything, bargaining and commissions in all things. Where is the answer? When will there be an explanation?

Jayalalithaa, one of those accused in the disproportionate-assets case, petitioned the Supreme Court insisting that a particular person of her choosing must remain the judge who hears and decides that case, and that a particular person of her choosing must remain the public prosecutor who is supposed to argue against her. All India is asking questions about this. Where is the lady's answer?
When will the Chief Minister try to answer all these questions? This is my explanation. These are the questions I raise!
With affection,
M. K.
12 November 2013
